problem_statement.Title: Top-level exposes only string __version__; no comparable version_info available
problem_statement.Description: The matplotlib top-level module exposes only __version__ as a string via a module-level __getattr__. There is no readily-importable, directly-comparable top-level version descriptor (for example, a version_info tuple or a parsed Version object) for consumers to use.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) lib/matplotlib/__init__.py defines a module-level __getattr__ that only handles the name "__version__" and returns a cached string computed via setuptools_scm.get_version(...) or the fallback _version.version; no top-level comparable representation is exposed. (lib/matplotlib/__init__.py, __getattr__, lines ~133-151)

2) The module contains no symbol or code path that exposes a parsed or tuple-based version descriptor (e.g., version_info). In other words, the module encodes no facility for returning a comparable top-level version attribute; consumers must parse or coerce mpl.__version__ themselves. (lib/matplotlib/__init__.py)

3) The packaging parser is already available in the module ("from packaging.version import parse as parse_version" at line ~105) but it is not used to populate any top-level parsed or comparable attribute. This shows a readily-available parsing utility is present but not used for top-level exposure. (lib/matplotlib/__init__.py, line ~105)

4) The __getattr__ logic uses a fallback value _version.version when setuptools_scm is not invoked; the repository snapshot inspected here does not contain lib/matplotlib/_version.py, so the exact fallback string format could not be inspected. The absence of the fallback file in this snapshot means assumptions about its PEP 440 compliance or exact string form are unverified. (reference: __getattr__ fallback to _version.version; lib/matplotlib/_version.py missing in snapshot)

5) The module does not define a module-level __dir__, so dynamically-provided attributes would not be advertised by dir(matplotlib). (lib/matplotlib/__init__.py — no def __dir__ present)

6) Investigation-identified computation (evidence-based suggested relationship for a comparable top-level descriptor):
   - Given the cached __version__ string, a robust comparable representation can be derived by parsing __version__ with packaging.version.parse(__version__) and using parsed.release (a tuple of ints) when present; if parsed.release is not available, extract digit groups from the string and convert to ints as a fallback (e.g., re.findall(r'\d+', __version__) -> tuple(int(...))). This relationship is an investigation finding for producing a version_info-like tuple from the available pieces (parse_version, __version__, numeric fallback). It is not implemented in the inspected code. (lib/matplotlib/__init__.py and use of parse_version)

7) Limitations and gaps observed in the snapshot: packaging.version.parse may return a LegacyVersion or other non-PEP440-parsed object for non-PEP440 strings; parsed.release may be absent for such returns. Additionally, reducing a parsed Version to its .release tuple intentionally drops pre/post/dev ordering information (for example, '3.5.0rc1' vs '3.5.0'). The repository snapshot inspection did not exercise these runtime cases, and lib/matplotlib/_version.py (the fallback) is missing here, so these behaviors remain to be validated with actual runtime values and tests. (investigation limitation)
StepsToReproduce: Open a Python REPL or run a one-liner; import matplotlib: import matplotlib as mpl
Inspect the exposed version string: print(mpl.__version__)
Try to access a comparable top-level attribute: print(mpl.version_info)  # raises AttributeError
Attempt a direct numeric comparison using the top-level value: print(mpl.__version__ >= (3, 1))  # demonstrates type mismatch / inability to compare directly to a tuple
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The matplotlib top-level module exposes a readily-importable, directly-comparable version descriptor (for example, matplotlib.version_info) that can be used in simple comparisons such as matplotlib.version_info >= (3, 1, 0).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Only matplotlib.__version__ (a string) is available at top-level. There is no matplotlib.version_info or equivalent comparable top-level attribute; consumers must parse or coerce mpl.__version__ themselves to perform numeric comparisons.

hints_text:
It seems that `__version_info__` is the way to go.

### Prior art
- There's no official specification for version tuples. [PEP 396 - Module Version Numbers](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0396/) only defines the string `__version__`.

- Many projects don't bother with version tuples.

- When they do, `__version_info__` seems to be a common thing:
  - [Stackoverflow discussion](https://stackoverflow.com/a/466694)
  - [PySide2](https://doc.qt.io/qtforpython-5.12/pysideversion.html#printing-project-and-qt-version) uses it.

- Python itself has the string [sys.version](https://docs.python.org/3/library/sys.html#sys.version) and the (named)tuple [sys.version_info](https://docs.python.org/3/library/sys.html#sys.version_info). In analogy to that `__version_info__` next to `__version__` makes sense for packages.

problem_statement.Title: RangeSlider initialization raises IndexError when valinit is degenerate (min == max)
problem_statement.Description: Creating a RangeSlider with an initial value pair where val[0] == val[1] (for example valinit=[0.0, 0.0]) causes RangeSlider.set_val to raise IndexError because the method writes to a fifth polygon vertex (xy[4]) that may not exist.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) lib/matplotlib/widgets.py::RangeSlider.set_val unconditionally writes to indices 0..4 of self.poly.xy (both orientation branches include an assignment to xy[4], e.g. "xy[4] = val[0], .25"). The method therefore assumes self.poly.xy always contains at least 5 vertices; when it does not, the write to xy[4] raises IndexError.

2) lib/matplotlib/patches.py::Polygon.set_xy appends a closing (duplicate) vertex only when the first and last vertices differ. The relevant behavior is:
   if self._closed:
       if nverts == 1 or (nverts > 1 and (xy[0] != xy[-1]).any()):
           xy = np.concatenate([xy, [xy[0]]])
   As a result, poly.xy may legitimately have 4 or 5 vertices depending on the input vertices; it is not guaranteed to include a duplicated closing vertex.

3) lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py::axvspan / axhspan construct the polygon with exactly four vertices (verts = [(xmin, ymin), (xmin, ymax), (xmax, ymax), (xmax, ymin)]). Because these creators pass 4 vertices, whether Polygon stores a fifth (closing) vertex depends solely on Polygon.set_xy's conditional behavior; therefore RangeSlider.set_val's unconditional write to xy[4] is fragile when the span is degenerate (e.g. xmin == xmax or ymin == ymax).

4) lib/matplotlib/widgets.py::Slider.set_val (a sibling implementation) does not assume a duplicated closing vertex and only updates indices that exist for both 4- and 5-vertex polygons (it does not reference xy[4]). This demonstrates a safe pattern that RangeSlider currently does not follow.

5) Immediate cause: the specific statement(s) in RangeSlider.set_val that lead to the reported IndexError are the assignments to xy[4] in both branches (horizontal and vertical), e.g. the line(s) "xy[4] = val[0], .25" / "xy[4] = .25, val[0]" in lib/matplotlib/widgets.py::RangeSlider.set_val.
StepsToReproduce:
Run a script that constructs a matplotlib RangeSlider with a degenerate initial range where min == max, for example:
  RangeSlider(ax, 'Threshold', data_min, data_max, valinit=[0.0, 0.0])
Observe that RangeSlider initialization calls set_val and raises an IndexError during construction.
Alternatively, reproduce interactively by creating a RangeSlider where the provided valinit has equal min and max.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: RangeSlider initializes cleanly with the provided initial values, including degenerate pairs where min == max. The widget should not raise an IndexError during construction, and the slider handles/visuals should reflect the user-supplied initial range.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When RangeSlider is constructed with a degenerate valinit (e.g. [0.0, 0.0]), an IndexError is raised during initialization: "IndexError: index 4 is out of bounds for axis 0 with size 4", originating from lib/matplotlib/widgets.py in RangeSlider.set_val when the code attempts to write xy[4] = (val[0], .25).

problem_statement.Title: Hovering AxesImage with BoundaryNorm crashes because Artist.format_cursor_data calls non-invertible norm.inverse
problem_statement.Description: When a ScalarMappable (e.g., AxesImage) uses a BoundaryNorm, moving the mouse over the image triggers Artist.format_cursor_data which calls norm.inverse without guarding for non-invertible norms. BoundaryNorm.inverse raises ValueError("BoundaryNorm is not invertible"), the exception propagates, and interactive cursor behavior stops/crashes.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) lib/matplotlib/artist.py::Artist.format_cursor_data assumes all Normalize subclasses implement an inverse and calls it unguarded. Exact expression in the file: neighbors = self.norm.inverse((int(self.norm(data) * n) + np.array([0, 1])) / n) (artist.py:1307-1308). This call is executed for scalar values when the artist is a ScalarMappable and the normalized value is finite, and there is no try/except protecting against exceptions from norm.inverse.

2) lib/matplotlib/colors.py::BoundaryNorm.inverse is intentionally non-invertible and unconditionally raises ValueError("BoundaryNorm is not invertible") (colors.py:1904-1912). BoundaryNorm maps continuous inputs to discrete bins and stores bin edges in self.boundaries (colors.py:1839), so it does not provide a bijective inverse; calling its inverse will raise.

3) The cursor-formatting logic in Artist.format_cursor_data depends on obtaining a pair of "neighbors" to compute a delta used for deciding the number of significant digits: the code computes delta = abs(neighbors - data).max() and then g_sig_digits = cbook._g_sig_digits(data, delta) before formatting (artist.py:1309-1310). Because neighbors is obtained via norm.inverse in the current code path, callers must not assume norm.inverse exists for all norm types if they need bracketing information for formatting.

4) Other callers that need inverse-like behavior already special-case BoundaryNorm and use stored boundaries to derive forward/inverse mappings instead of calling norm.inverse. For example, lib/matplotlib/colorbar.py checks for isinstance(self.norm, colors.BoundaryNorm) or self.boundaries is not None and then derives forward/inverse behavior from boundaries instead of calling self.norm.inverse (colorbar.py:1188-1191, 1236-1240, 1260-1266). This demonstrates the appropriate diagnostic: callers that require neighbor/bracketing information must not assume norm.inverse is available for all Normalize subclasses and should use boundaries (when present) to obtain the bracketing edges (the previous and next boundary that bracket the data value) to compute delta; if boundaries are not present, the existing numeric fallback precision present in artist.format_cursor_data (3 significant digits) is the fallback currently used (artist.py:1311-1313).
StepsToReproduce: Run the following Python snippet:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import matplotlib as mpl
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
norm = mpl.colors.BoundaryNorm(np.linspace(-4, 4, 5), 256)
X = np.random.randn(10, 10)
pc = ax.imshow(X, cmap='RdBu_r', norm=norm)
Display the figure and move the mouse cursor over the image (hover).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Hovering the mouse over the image should not raise an exception or stop interactive cursor behavior. The cursor/tooltip should show a formatted scalar value (or appropriate representation for binned data) and use a reasonable number of significant digits. For binned/discrete norms (BoundaryNorm), formatting should be based on the bin boundaries or otherwise fall back to a safe default precision instead of causing an exception.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Hovering over the image raises ValueError: "BoundaryNorm is not invertible" (raised from lib/matplotlib/colors.py::BoundaryNorm.inverse) called by lib/matplotlib/artist.py::Artist.format_cursor_data, and interactive cursor behavior stops.

(In my initial suggestion one would have to check if `ends` maps to (0,1) and if `normed` is in [0, 1] for the correct way to implement my idea. But these conditions are taylored to catch `BoundaryNorm` anyway, so why not do it directly.)

```python
# Midpoints of neighboring color intervals.
try:
    neighbors = self.norm.inverse(
        (int(normed * n) + np.array([0, 1])) / n)
except ValueError:
    # non-invertible ScalarMappable
```
because `BoundaryNorm` raises a `ValueError` anyway and I assume this to be the case for all non-invertible `ScalarMappables`.
(And the issue is the exception raised from `inverse`).

The question is:
What to put in the `except`? So what is "acting accordingly" in this case?
If `BoundaryNorm` isn't invertible, how can we reliably get the data values of the midpoints of neighboring colors?

I think it should be enough to get the boundaries themselves, instead of the midpoints (though both is possible) with something like:
```python
cur_idx = np.argmin(np.abs(self.norm.boundaries - data))
cur_bound = self.norm.boundaries[cur_idx]
neigh_idx = cur_idx + 1 if data > cur_bound else cur_idx - 1
neighbors = self.norm.boundaries[
    np.array([cur_idx, neigh_idx])
]
```
Problems with this code are:
- `boundaries` property is specific to `BoundaryNorm`, isn't it? So we gained nothing by using `try .. except`
- more checks are needed to cover all cases for `clip` and `extend` options of `BoundaryNorm` such that `neigh_idx` is always in bounds
- for very coarse boundaries compared to data (ie: `boundaries = [0,1,2,3,4,5]; data = random(n)*5) the displayed values are always rounded to one significant digit. While in principle, this is expected (I believe), it results in inconsistency. In my example 0.111111 would be given as 0.1 and 0.001 as 0.001 and 1.234 would be just 1.
> boundaries property is specific to BoundaryNorm, isn't it? So we gained nothing by using try .. except

This is one argument in favor of doing the `isinstance` check because then you can assert we _have_ a BoundaryNorm and can trust that you can access its state etc.   One on hand, duck typeing is in general a Good Thing in Python and we should err on the side of being forgiving on input, but sometimes the complexity of it is more trouble than it is worth if you really only have 1 case that you are trying catch!  

>  I assume this to be the case for all non-invertible ScalarMappables.

However we only (at this point) are talking about a way to recover in the case of BoundaryNorm.  Let everything else continue to fail and we will deal with those issues if/when they get reported.

problem_statement.Title: matplotlib.get_backend() can clear figures from Gcf when rcParams['backend'] is the auto-backend sentinel
problem_statement.Description: Calling matplotlib.get_backend() can remove all figures from matplotlib._pylab_helpers.Gcf.figs when the stored rcParams['backend'] value is the auto-backend sentinel (rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel). The removal manifests as Gcf.figs becoming an empty OrderedDict and prevents subsequent operations such as plt.close(fig) from finding the figure manager.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) lib/matplotlib/__init__.py::RcParams.__getitem__ performs a backend switch as a side effect when the key is "backend" and the stored value is rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel. The code path is:
   - In RcParams.__getitem__: when key == "backend" and self is the global rcParams, if dict.__getitem__(self, 'backend') is rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel then it does: "from matplotlib import pyplot as plt; plt.switch_backend(rcsetup._auto_backend_sentinel)" (lib/matplotlib/__init__.py lines ~673-679). This makes reading rcParams['backend'] via RcParams.__getitem__ destructive.

2) lib/matplotlib/__init__.py::get_backend is implemented as a direct read of rcParams['backend'] ("return rcParams['backend']"). Because RcParams.__getitem__ can call plt.switch_backend (see 1), calling get_backend() can trigger a backend switch and thus have destructive side effects (lib/matplotlib/__init__.py lines ~1170-1178).

3) lib/matplotlib/pyplot.py::switch_backend begins by calling close("all"). The close("all") call leads to _pylab_helpers.Gcf.destroy_all(), which destroys managers and clears the internal OrderedDict of figures. Therefore, an inadvertent call to switch_backend (e.g. triggered from RcParams.__getitem__) will clear Gcf.figs (pyplot.switch_backend: close("all") at lib/matplotlib/pyplot.py lines ~222-226; Gcf.destroy_all clears cls.figs, lib/matplotlib/_pylab_helpers.py lines ~83-92).

4) lib/matplotlib/_pylab_helpers.py::Gcf.destroy_all empties the class-level OrderedDict Gcf.figs ("cls.figs.clear()"), which is the exact operation observable in the repro (before: OrderedDict with managers; after: OrderedDict()). This is why figures disappear from Gcf after the side-effecting read (lib/matplotlib/_pylab_helpers.py lines ~83-92).

5) Other code in the codebase deliberately bypasses the side-effecting RcParams.__getitem__ by using dict.__getitem__(rcParams, 'backend') (for example, pyplot._get_backend_mod uses dict.__getitem__ to avoid importing pyplot and triggering switch_backend). There is also a RcParams._get_backend_or_none() helper that returns None if the stored value is the auto sentinel using dict.__getitem__. These facts indicate that raw dict access is relied on elsewhere to avoid RcParams.__getitem__'s side effect (lib/matplotlib/pyplot.py lines ~195-206; lib/matplotlib/__init__.py lines ~681-685).
StepsToReproduce: Run Python with a Matplotlib installation where rcParams['backend'] is the auto-backend sentinel (the default stored value when the backend is not explicitly selected).
Execute the sequence: import matplotlib.pyplot as plt; from matplotlib import get_backend, rc_context
Create a figure inside an rc_context(): with rc_context(): fig2 = plt.figure()
Capture the Gcf.figs state before: before = f'{id(plt._pylab_helpers.Gcf)} {plt._pylab_helpers.Gcf.figs!r}'
Call get_backend(): get_backend()
Capture the Gcf.figs state after similarly and compare; observe that the figures previously present are removed (before != after).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Querying the current backend via matplotlib.get_backend() (or reading rcParams['backend'] via RcParams.__getitem__) should be a non-destructive query: it must not remove or destroy existing figures. Gcf.figs should remain unchanged and per-figure operations such as plt.close(fig) should continue to work after calling get_backend().
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling matplotlib.get_backend() (which reads rcParams['backend']) can remove all figures from Gcf.figs. In the provided reproduction the 'before' state is e.g. 'OrderedDict([(1, <matplotlib.backends.backend_qt.FigureManagerQT object ...>)])' and the 'after' state is 'OrderedDict()'; consequent operations like plt.close(fig) fail to find the figure manager.

hints_text:
My knee-jerk guess is that  :  

 - the `rcParams['backend']` in the auto-sentinel
 - that is stashed by rc_context
 - if you do the first thing to force the backend to be resolved in the context manager it get changes
 - the context manager sets it back to the sentinel an the way out
 - `get_backend()` re-resolves the backend which because it changes the backend it closes all of the figures


This is probably been a long standing latent bug, but was brought to the front when we made the backend resolution lazier.

problem_statement.Title: Axes3D.set_visible(False) ignored — 3D subplot remains visible
problem_statement.Description: Calling ax.set_visible(False) on a subplot with projection='3d' does not hide the subplot: the 3D axes and their contents continue to be drawn.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/axes3d.py :: Axes3D.draw performs 3D-specific drawing unconditionally and does not check the Axes visibility before drawing. The method begins drawing (calls self._unstale_viewLim(), self.patch.draw(renderer), projects collections/patches, and calls axis.draw(renderer)) with no early-return guard (i.e., no "if not self.get_visible(): return"), so ax.set_visible(False) on an Axes3D instance does not prevent these drawing steps.

2) lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/axis3d.py :: Axis.draw lacks an early visibility guard. Axis.draw executes a full sequence of rendering steps (line/label/pane/grid/tick drawing) without first checking self.get_visible(), whereas other Axis methods (see next item) are visibility-aware. This means an Axis that has been set invisible can still be rendered if callers invoke axis.draw(renderer) without first checking axis visibility.

3) lib/matplotlib/axes/_base.py :: _AxesBase.draw enforces the Artist contract by beginning with an early visibility check: "if not self.get_visible(): return". The Axes3D.draw override deviates from this contract by performing drawing before delegating to the base-class draw, so the expected axes-level visibility short-circuit does not occur for 3D axes.

4) lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/axis3d.py :: Axis.get_tightbbox already checks visibility (it starts with "if not self.get_visible(): return") demonstrating inconsistent handling within the same Axis class between visibility-aware measurement/layout methods and the draw method which lacks the same guard.
StepsToReproduce: Run a script that creates two 3D subplots, plots on both, calls ax1.set_visible(False), and shows the figure. Example:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, subplot_kw={'projection': '3d'})
ax1.scatter(1,1,1)
ax2.scatter(1,1,1, c='r')
ax1.set_visible(False)
plt.show()
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When ax.set_visible(False) is called on a 3D subplot, that subplot (axes frame, ticks, labels, and plotted artists for that axes) is not drawn and does not appear in the displayed figure.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: After ax.set_visible(False) on a subplot with projection='3d', the subplot remains visible and its 3D contents (axes, ticks, labels, plotted points) continue to be drawn.

problem_statement.Title: Figure dpi doubles on each pickle/unpickle on macOS (Retina) causing OverflowError
problem_statement.Description: When a matplotlib Figure is pickled and then unpickled on macOS (using the MacOSX backend), the figure.dpi value is increased by the display device pixel ratio on every unpickle. Repeating pickle/unpickle cycles multiplies the dpi each time (e.g. 200 -> 400 -> 800 ...), eventually causing an OverflowError in backend canvas initialization.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) lib/matplotlib/figure.py::Figure.__getstate__ — Figure.__getstate__ calls super().__getstate__() which copies the Figure's __dict__ and then only removes the 'canvas' key. Because runtime/backend-managed attributes that may exist on the Figure (e.g. '_original_dpi') are left in the dict, these runtime-only values are persisted into the pickle state.

2) lib/matplotlib/figure.py::Figure.__setstate__ — Figure.__setstate__ restores the saved state by assigning self.__dict__ = state and only afterwards constructs a canvas via FigureCanvasBase(self). Restoring the full dict before reinitializing the backend means any persisted dpi-related values and caches are present when the canvas constructor runs.

3) lib/matplotlib/backend_bases.py::FigureCanvasBase.__init__ — the canvas constructor unconditionally records the current figure.dpi into a runtime cache by doing: figure._original_dpi = figure.dpi. This assignment assumes figure.dpi currently represents the unscaled base DPI; when unpickling restores a figure whose dpi is already the result of prior device scaling, this overwrites any original base-DPI cache with a previously-scaled value.

4) lib/matplotlib/backend_bases.py::FigureCanvasBase._set_device_pixel_ratio — device-scaling logic computes the figure DPI as: dpi_new = device_pixel_ratio * self.figure._original_dpi and then calls self.figure._set_dpi(dpi_new, forward=False). If figure._original_dpi holds an already-scaled value (because of the restore + canvas ctor ordering), multiplying by the device_pixel_ratio applies the scaling multiplicatively, producing the observed doubling sequence.

5) lib/matplotlib/backends/backend_macosx.py::FigureCanvasMac.__init__ (and the C extension _macosx.FigureCanvas.__init__) — the macOS backend class participates in canvas construction (via super().__init__(figure=figure)) and the C-level canvas initialization is invoked during unpickle (shown in the user stack trace). The C extension is on the MRO and thus runs during canvas init; that initialization is where device_pixel_ratio is determined and the _set_device_pixel_ratio path is exercised, producing the user-visible doubling and eventual OverflowError when pixel sizes exceed integer limits.

6) Concrete numeric relationship and symptom — the code path uses the formula dpi_new = device_pixel_ratio * figure._original_dpi. On Retina macOS with device_pixel_ratio == 2, repeated unpickle cycles that restore a previously-scaled state lead to the recurrence dpi_{n+1} = 2 * dpi_n (200.0 -> 400.0 -> 800.0 -> ...), which explains the exponential growth and the subsequent OverflowError during backend canvas initialization.

7) Related code paths — save/print logic may consult '_original_dpi' via getattr(self.figure, '_original_dpi', self.figure.dpi), demonstrating that multiple code paths rely on the presence/semantics of _original_dpi; restoring or persisting runtime-only DPI caches therefore affects other operations that expect a consistent meaning for that attribute.
StepsToReproduce: On an M1 Mac (macOS) using the MacOSX backend, run a Python script that: (a) creates a matplotlib.figure.Figure, (b) pickles the Figure to a file, (c) unpickles the Figure from the file, and (d) repeats steps (b)-(c) in a loop while printing fig.dpi after each unpickle.
Observe that fig.dpi is multiplied by the device pixel ratio on each unpickle (e.g., 200, 400, 800, ...) and that continuing causes an OverflowError in the backend canvas initialization.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: After unpickling a Figure, its dpi remains the same as it was when pickled (user-visible sizes and resolution do not change). Repeated pickle/unpickle cycles do not alter the figure.dpi or cause backend overflow errors.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Each unpickle multiplies figure.dpi by the device pixel ratio (on Retina macOS this appears as doubling). The dpi increases multiplicatively across iterations (200.0, 400.0, 800.0, ...), and repeated cycles eventually raise an OverflowError during backend canvas initialization (for example, 'OverflowError: signed integer is greater than maximum').

problem_statement.Title: 'Poly3DCollection' object has no attribute '_facecolors2d' when calling get_facecolors before draw
problem_statement.Description: Calling get_facecolors() (or get_facecolor()) on a Poly3DCollection object before the 3D projection/draw step raises an AttributeError because the collection's 2D-projected color attributes (_facecolors2d/_edgecolors2d) do not exist until projection runs.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Poly3DCollection.get_facecolor and Poly3DCollection.get_edgecolor unconditionally return self._facecolors2d / self._edgecolors2d. Incorrect assumption: _facecolors2d and _edgecolors2d always exist because projection has already run. Location: lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py :: class Poly3DCollection :: get_facecolor / get_edgecolor (see functions at lines where they simply 'return self._facecolors2d' and 'return self._edgecolors2d').

2) do_3d_projection is the code path that computes and assigns the 2D-projected arrays self._facecolors2d and self._edgecolors2d. The getters therefore depend on an attribute that is created only during projection/draw. Location: lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py :: Poly3DCollection.do_3d_projection (see the lines where self._facecolors2d and self._edgecolors2d are assigned from the depth-sorted results).

3) Poly3DCollection stores unsorted 3D color and alpha state in _facecolor3d, _edgecolor3d, and _alpha3d (initialized by set_3d_properties and updated by set_facecolor/set_edgecolor). These *_3d attributes are available before projection and represent a reasonable, unsorted color representation for callers that query colors pre-draw. Location: lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py :: Poly3DCollection.set_3d_properties, set_facecolor, set_edgecolor.

4) The base class PolyCollection provides get_facecolor and get_edgecolor that return the collection's stored (unsorted) color arrays and includes the 'face' special-case for edge colors. These base-class getters are used by Poly3DCollection to initialize its *_3d state and therefore represent the existing unsorted/3D-less color representation. Location: lib/matplotlib/collections.py :: class PolyCollection :: get_facecolor / get_edgecolor.

5) The user-visible failure mode is caused by the logical relationship being violated: callers of the getters may run before do_3d_projection has executed. The getters should not unconditionally assume the projected attributes exist; instead, callers querying colors before a draw currently encounter AttributeError. Location: user reproduction path via lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/axes3d.py :: Axes3D.plot_surface (which constructs Poly3DCollection and sets face/edge colors) followed by calling the collection's get_facecolor/get_facecolors before a draw.

6) Naming/version note: the user's traceback invoked get_facecolors (plural) while the current repository defines the singular getters get_facecolor/get_edgecolor on Poly3DCollection. This indicates a difference between the user's installed copy of mpl_toolkits.mplot3d.art3d and the current tree; I could not inspect the user's site-packages file to reconcile line numbers or exact API differences. Location: user's traceback vs lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py in the repository.
StepsToReproduce: Run a Python session with matplotlib installed (example used mpl 1.3.1 and 1.4.2).

Execute:

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D

y,x = np.ogrid[1:10:100j, 1:10:100j]
z2 = np.cos(x)**3 - np.sin(y)**2
fig = plt.figure()
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='3d')
r = ax.plot_surface(x, y, z2, cmap='hot')
r.get_facecolors()
Observe the exception raised on the last line.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Calling get_facecolors() or get_facecolor() on a Poly3DCollection returns an array of RGBA colors (numpy array) representing the collection's face colors and does not raise an exception before a draw. After a draw or projection step, the getters should return the projected/sorted RGBA array for the visible 2D representation.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling r.get_facecolors() raises AttributeError: 'Poly3DCollection' object has no attribute '_facecolors2d'. The traceback shows the error originates in art3d.py's getter which attempts to return self._facecolors2d before do_3d_projection has created it.

The problem still is that we update some state during the draw process.  Internally we do the updating and the access in just the right order so that in most cases we do not notice that we also add the attributes on the fly. 

The best workaround for the current case (assuming 3.5):

fig.draw_without_rendering()   # <--- This is the key line

problem_statement.Title: Conversion helpers set __class__ before 3D init — partial conversion leaves Line3D without _verts3d causing AttributeError
problem_statement.Description: When converting 2D artists to 3D, mpl_toolkits.mplot3d conversion helpers assign the object's __class__ to a 3D subclass before running the 3D-specific initialization. If that initialization raises (for example a numpy broadcasting error), the object remains an instance of the 3D class but lacks required 3D attributes (notably _verts3d). This later causes an AttributeError during drawing (Line3D.draw) and the corrupted object can persist in the session until the interpreter/kernel is restarted.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Multiple conversion helper functions in lib/mpl_toolkits/mplot3d/art3d.py mutate the object's class to a 3D subclass before performing 3D initialization. Examples from the file (assignment then init call):
   - text_2d_to_3d: obj.__class__ = Text3D; obj.set_3d_properties(z, zdir) (art3d.py lines ~153-156).
   - line_2d_to_3d: line.__class__ = Line3D; line.set_3d_properties(zs, zdir) (art3d.py lines ~221-226).
   - line_collection_2d_to_3d: col.__class__ = Line3DCollection; col.set_segments(segments3d) (art3d.py lines ~315-320).
   - patch_2d_to_3d: patch.__class__ = Patch3D; patch.set_3d_properties(verts, z, zdir) (art3d.py lines ~379-384).
   - pathpatch_2d_to_3d: pathpatch.__class__ = PathPatch3D; pathpatch.set_3d_properties(mpath, z, zdir) (art3d.py lines ~386-393).
   - patch_collection_2d_to_3d: col.__class__ = Path3DCollection or Patch3DCollection; col.set_3d_properties(zs, zdir) (art3d.py lines ~636-661).
   - poly_collection_2d_to_3d: col.__class__ = Poly3DCollection; col.set_verts_and_codes(...); col.set_3d_properties() (art3d.py lines ~876-883).
   Wrong assumption encoded in these helpers: it is safe to set obj.__class__ to the 3D subclass before completing the 3D-specific initialization.

2) The 3D initialization routines can raise on malformed inputs before they set the required 3D attributes. Concretely, Line3D.set_3d_properties performs:
   xs = self.get_xdata(); ys = self.get_ydata(); zs = np.broadcast_to(zs, len(xs)); self._verts3d = juggle_axes(xs, ys, zs, zdir) (art3d.py lines ~171-176).
   Wrong assumption encoded in this method: initialization will always succeed; in reality np.broadcast_to or juggle_axes may raise (for example, a numpy broadcasting error when zs has incompatible shape), which aborts initialization before _verts3d is assigned.

3) The drawing code for 3D classes assumes the required 3D attributes exist. For example, Line3D.draw unconditionally unpacks self._verts3d via "xs3d, ys3d, zs3d = self._verts3d" (art3d.py lines ~212-215). Wrong assumption: draw assumes any Line3D instance has a populated _verts3d attribute; if initialization failed earlier, this raises AttributeError during rendering (as observed in the user's traceback).

4) The ordering bug is present across all listed conversion helpers and their corresponding initialization routines (set_3d_properties, set_segments, set_verts_and_codes, etc.). Each occurrence encodes the same risky assumption and thus any of these helpers can leave a partially-converted object in the interpreter if their initialization raises. The functions and init routines listed in (1) must be considered equally affected.
StepsToReproduce: Prepare arrays/values for x_s_0, x_e_0, y_s_0, y_e_0, z_s_0, z_e_0 such that at least one provided zs value triggers a broadcasting/shape error in Line3D.set_3d_properties (e.g., pass a numpy array with unexpected dimensions for zs so that np.broadcast_to(zs, len(xs)) raises).
Run the example plotting code that calls ax.plot([...], [...], [...], clr_list) (the user's original snippet) so conversion helpers are invoked and a conversion raises during initialization.
Observe the first run raising a numpy broadcasting error (like: "input operand has more dimensions than allowed by the axis remapping").
Without restarting the kernel, adjust the input variables to simple ints or shapes that would normally succeed and re-run the plotting code.
Observe an AttributeError: 'Line3D' object has no attribute '_verts3d' raised during plt.show() / draw; the corrupted object persists in the session until the interpreter/kernel is restarted.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: 3D lines render when valid numeric inputs are provided. A failed conversion attempt (an exception raised during conversion) should not leave a long-lived, partially-converted object in the user's session that later causes AttributeError in rendering.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: If a 3D conversion raises during its initialization (for example due to a numpy broadcasting error in set_3d_properties), the object can remain with its __class__ changed to a 3D subclass but without required 3D attributes (notably _verts3d). Later rendering attempts raise AttributeError: 'Line3D' object has no attribute '_verts3d' (traceback indicates failure at Line3D.draw unpacking self._verts3d). The corrupted state can persist in the interpreter/kernel until restarted.

problem_statement.Title: Legend constructor does not accept 'draggable' kwargs (cannot make legend draggable at creation)
problem_statement.Description: Legend.__init__ (lib/matplotlib/legend.py) does not accept draggable-related constructor keywords (for example: draggable, draggable_use_blit, draggable_update). As a result, callers cannot enable dragging at creation (e.g. ax.legend(draggable=True) raises a TypeError). The public API for enabling dragging is present as Legend.set_draggable(...), but it must be called after construction.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Legend.__init__ does not accept draggable-related constructor arguments. Location: lib/matplotlib/legend.py:Legend.__init__ (constructor signature lines ~306–346). Evidence: the __init__ parameter list contains no parameters named draggable, draggable_use_blit, or draggable_update; self._draggable is only initialized later in __init__ (self._draggable = None at line ~540).

2) Enabling dragging is implemented via a separate method, not via constructor parameters. Location: lib/matplotlib/legend.py:set_draggable and get_draggable (lines ~1079–1115). Evidence: set_draggable(self, state, use_blit=False, update='loc') creates a DraggableLegend(self, use_blit, update=update) when enabling dragging; get_draggable() returns whether self._draggable is set.

3) DraggableLegend validates its 'update' parameter and depends on the legend's offsetbox. Location: lib/matplotlib/legend.py:DraggableLegend.__init__ (lines ~53–75). Evidence: DraggableLegend calls _api.check_in_list(["loc","bbox"], update=update) and then calls super().__init__(legend, legend._legend_box, use_blit=use_blit), so it relies on legend._legend_box being initialized.

4) The legend's offsetbox/figure/offset state required by the draggable helper is initialized during __init__, but __init__ does not call set_draggable. Location: lib/matplotlib/legend.py:_init_legend_box and _set_loc (lines ~723–821 and ~588–596). Evidence: _init_legend_box constructs self._legend_box and calls self._legend_box.set_figure(self.figure) (lines ~812–819); _set_loc sets the legend box offset via self._legend_box.set_offset(self._findoffset) (line ~595). Meanwhile, DraggableOffsetBox (lib/matplotlib/offsetbox.py) uses offsetbox.figure and offset values (save_offset/get_loc_in_canvas at lines ~1593–1611).

5) Axes.legend and Figure.legend forward keyword arguments to Legend, so the missing constructor keywords manifest as an API gap at the pyplot/axes level. Location: lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py:Axes.legend (lines ~300–309) and lib/matplotlib/figure.py:Figure.legend (lines ~1102–1120). Evidence: both construct mlegend.Legend(..., **kwargs), so passing draggable to ax.legend currently is rejected by Legend.__init__.

6) Documentation and tests do not document or exercise making a legend draggable at construction. Location: lib/matplotlib/legend.py:_legend_kw_doc and lib/matplotlib/tests/test_legend.py. Evidence: _legend_kw_doc (legend keyword documentation) does not include draggable-related kwargs; tests/test_legend.py contains test_get_set_draggable() that exercises set_draggable toggling (lines ~777–783) but no test for constructor-enabled dragging.

7) Backwards-compatibility considerations were not examined in external code. Location: lib/matplotlib/legend.py:Legend.__init__. Evidence: the investigation did not search for third-party code that might depend on the exact Legend.__init__ signature; however, Axes.legend/Figure.legend already forward kwargs so the change would primarily be additive within Matplotlib's API surface.
StepsToReproduce: python -c "import matplotlib.pyplot as plt; fig, ax = plt.subplots(); ax.legend(['a'], draggable=True)"
Alternatively: from matplotlib.legend import Legend; Legend(ax, ['a'], draggable=True)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Passing draggable=True (and optional parameters for blit/update) to Legend at creation enables dragging immediately: the legend is draggable without a separate set_draggable call, and legend.get_draggable() returns True. ax.legend(draggable=True) should accept the kwarg without raising an unexpected-keyword TypeError.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Legend.__init__ does not accept draggable-related kwargs. Calling ax.legend(draggable=True) raises a TypeError for an unexpected keyword argument (or otherwise rejects the kwarg), and the legend remains non-draggable until set_draggable(...) is called after construction.

problem_statement.Title: PostScript backend raises TypeError when text contains an empty line (None appended to stream)
problem_statement.Description: Saving a figure with the PostScript backend fails with a TypeError when the figure contains a multi-line text label that includes an empty line. The failure occurs during text drawing in the PostScript renderer.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) lib/matplotlib/backends/backend_ps.py :: RendererPS.draw_text — The method builds a list named stream by collecting per-font chunks into a transient variable curr_stream. After iterating over _text_helpers.layout(s, font) it unconditionally does stream.append(curr_stream). This encodes the incorrect assumption that the layout iterator yields at least one item; if layout yields nothing (e.g., when s == ''), curr_stream remains None and a None is appended to stream. Later code unpacks stream elements with "for ps_name, xs_names in stream:" and raises TypeError when encountering that None.

2) lib/matplotlib/_text_helpers.py :: layout — layout yields one LayoutItem per character in the input string (it iterates "for char in string: yield LayoutItem(...)"). For an empty string the iterator yields no items. This behavior means callers must handle an empty iterator; it is not a bug in layout itself.

3) lib/matplotlib/text.py :: Text.draw / Text._get_layout — Multi-line text is split with text.split("\n") (preserving empty lines) and the drawing loop passes per-line clean_line values into the renderer via renderer.draw_text. As a result, the PS renderer can receive s == '' for empty lines in multi-line text.

4) lib/matplotlib/backends/backend_pdf.py :: RendererPdf.draw_text (comparison evidence) — the PDF renderer consumes _text_helpers.layout and constructs chunk lists only when items are present (it appends chunks conditionally). The PDF backend therefore does not append a None-like chunk for empty input and demonstrates the safe-chunking behavior that the PS renderer lacks.
StepsToReproduce: Create a Python script with the following content:
from matplotlib.figure import Figure
figure = Figure()
ax = figure.add_subplot(111)
ax.annotate(text='\nLower label', xy=(0, 0))
figure.savefig('figure.eps')
Run the script with the PostScript backend (e.g., default .eps output) using Python 3.9 and matplotlib 3.6.0.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The figure is saved to 'figure.eps' successfully without raising an exception; multi-line text labels that include empty lines are rendered and saved normally.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Saving raises a TypeError: 'cannot unpack non-iterable NoneType object'. The traceback points to backend_ps.py during text rendering where code attempts 'for ps_name, xs_names in stream:' and encounters a None entry in stream.

problem_statement.Title: UserWarning about incompatible layout emitted when constrained_layout=False (constructor treats False as enable)
problem_statement.Description: Passing constrained_layout=False (or tight_layout=False) to Figure/plt.subplots still installs the corresponding layout engine. As a result, calls to plt.subplots_adjust or tight_layout emit a UserWarning about an incompatible layout engine even though the user explicitly passed False.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) lib/matplotlib/figure.py:Figure.__init__ — The constructor uses `is not None` to decide whether to install a layout engine. The branches `elif tight_layout is not None:` and `elif constrained_layout is not None:` call self.set_layout_engine(layout='tight'/'constrained'), so passing constrained_layout=False (or tight_layout=False) still installs the corresponding layout engine (because False is not None).

2) lib/matplotlib/figure.py:FigureBase.subplots_adjust — subplots_adjust checks the presence and compatibility of the current layout engine and emits the warning if an engine exists and is incompatible: it warns when `self.get_layout_engine() is not None and not self.get_layout_engine().adjust_compatible` (this is the exact condition that produces the observed UserWarning).

3) lib/matplotlib/layout_engine.py:ConstrainedLayoutEngine and TightLayoutEngine — The engine compatibility flags are encoded as class attributes: `ConstrainedLayoutEngine._adjust_compatible = False` and `TightLayoutEngine._adjust_compatible = True`. Because a ConstrainedLayoutEngine has `_adjust_compatible = False`, its presence triggers the incompatible-layout warning in subplots_adjust.

4) lib/matplotlib/figure.py:Figure.set_constrained_layout and Figure.set_tight_layout — The setter methods convert their argument to bool and only install the engine when the argument is truthy (they use `bool(...)` to gate installation). The constructor's `is not None` check is inconsistent with these setters: the constructor installs the engine when the parameter is not None (so False installs), while the setters install only when the parameter is truthy.

5) lib/matplotlib/figure.py:Figure.__init__ (tight_layout branch) — The tight_layout branch uses the same `is not None` logic as constrained_layout, so tight_layout=False similarly results in installing TightLayoutEngine in the constructor. Because TightLayoutEngine._adjust_compatible is True, this case would not produce the same subplots_adjust warning, but the constructor behavior is the same pattern and therefore included in the root cause.

6) pyplot interaction — matplotlib.pyplot.subplots forwards figure keyword arguments (fig_kw) to Figure.__init__, so passing `constrained_layout=False` to plt.subplots triggers the constructor path above and leads to the observed warning.
StepsToReproduce: Run the following in a Python session with Matplotlib 3.6.0:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
a = np.linspace(0,2*np.pi,100)
b = np.sin(a); c = np.cos(a)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(1,2,figsize=(8,2), constrained_layout=False)
ax[0].plot(a,b); ax[1].plot(a,c)
plt.subplots_adjust(wspace=0)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: No UserWarning should be emitted when the user explicitly sets constrained_layout=False (or tight_layout=False). subplots_adjust and tight_layout calls should run normally when the user has disabled the corresponding layout engine.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: A UserWarning is emitted stating: "This figure was using a layout engine that is incompatible with subplots_adjust and/or tight_layout; not calling subplots_adjust." This occurs even though constrained_layout=False was passed and the user expected no layout engine to be active.

problem_statement.Title: ax.bar raises StopIteration for all-NaN x inputs (matplotlib 3.6.1)
problem_statement.Description: Calling Axes.bar (and some other Axes methods) with x (and/or related) sequences that contain only non-finite values (e.g. [np.nan]) can raise StopIteration. The exception originates because callers probe the "first finite" element via cbook._safe_first_finite, which raises StopIteration when no finite element exists, and several Axes call sites do not handle that StopIteration.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) lib/matplotlib/cbook/__init__.py::_safe_first_finite uses next(...) over a generator (return next(val for val in obj if safe_isfinite(val))). If the iterable contains no finite values (for example [np.nan], or an iterable whose elements are all non-finite), _safe_first_finite raises StopIteration. Callers of this helper must therefore treat the "no finite element" case as a possible outcome.

2) lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py:Axes._convert_dx calls cbook._safe_first_finite(x0) and cbook._safe_first_finite(xconv) while only catching (TypeError, IndexError, KeyError) around those calls (the except clauses do not include StopIteration). When x (or the supplied inputs) contain no finite element, _safe_first_finite raises StopIteration which propagates out of _convert_dx and can cause ax.bar to abort with StopIteration.

3) lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py:broken_barh calls cbook._safe_first_finite(xranges) and cbook._safe_first_finite(yrange) without guarding StopIteration. These call sites share the same probe-for-first-finite-element pattern and thus are susceptible to StopIteration for all-nonfinite inputs.

4) lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py:_upcast_err (errorbar internals) calls cbook._safe_first_finite(err) inline (used inside isinstance(...) and type(...)) without handling StopIteration; an err sequence with only non-finite values will result in StopIteration propagating from this probe.

5) lib/matplotlib/axes/_axes.py:_parse_scatter_color_args (scatter) calls cbook._safe_first_finite(c) inline to detect the element type (e.g., whether the first element is a string) without handling StopIteration; a color sequence leading _safe_first_finite to raise StopIteration will cause scatter to raise.

6) Some other modules already handle StopIteration defensively: lib/matplotlib/dates.py:default_units and lib/matplotlib/units.py:Registry.get_converter both call cbook._safe_first_finite inside try/except that includes StopIteration. This shows mixed handling across the codebase and confirms the pattern (probe first finite element) is used in multiple places.

7) Downstream autoscaling and patch-limit update code (lib/matplotlib/axes/_base.py:update_datalim and related _update_patch_limits) explicitly check for finite coordinates (e.g., return if not np.any(np.isfinite(xys))) and thus tolerate NaN coordinates; therefore the observable crash is due to the unguarded probing callers raising StopIteration, not an inability of downstream code to handle NaNs.

8) Investigation gap: the original investigator did not run runtime tests after inspecting the code to confirm end-to-end behavior for all affected call sites; there may be additional callers of _safe_first_finite elsewhere that similarly lack StopIteration handling.
StepsToReproduce: python -c "import numpy as np; import matplotlib.pyplot as plt; f, ax = plt.subplots(); ax.bar([np.nan], [np.nan])"
Alternatively, in an interactive session:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
f, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.bar([np.nan], [np.nan])
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Calling ax.bar with x and/or height sequences that contain only NaN should return a BarCollection (with Rectangle(s) whose x and/or height are NaN) and should not raise StopIteration. More generally, API calls should not raise StopIteration for iterables containing only non-finite elements; caller-side probes for a "first finite" element should treat the absence of such an element as a valid outcome rather than allowing StopIteration to escape to the user.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling ax.bar([np.nan], [np.nan]) raises StopIteration. problem_statement.Title: KeyError accessing legacy seaborn style via plt.style.library on Matplotlib 3.6.1
problem_statement.Description: Direct dictionary lookup plt.style.library['seaborn-colorblind'] raises KeyError on Matplotlib 3.6.1 while style.use('seaborn-colorblind') continues to work (with a deprecation warning). The error occurs because the style library dictionary does not contain the legacy seaborn-style name that use() accepts and remaps at call time.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) reload_library populates the module-level library dictionary from installed .mplstyle filenames (file stems). read_style_directory uses path.stem as the key when building the base library, and reload_library assigns library = update_user_library(_base_library) then available[:] = sorted(library.keys()). Location: lib/matplotlib/style/core.py (read_style_directory, _base_library, reload_library). Evidence: read_style_directory sets styles[path.stem] = rc_params_from_file(...); reload_library sets library = update_user_library(_base_library) and available[:] = sorted(library.keys()).

2) There is no code in reload_library (or nearby) that creates backward-compatible legacy-name aliases (for example, 'seaborn-colorblind'). As a result, legacy names derived from older seaborn-style naming are absent from plt.style.library. Location: lib/matplotlib/style/core.py:reload_library. Evidence: reload_library contains only assignment of library and available and does not add new keys after reading style directories.

3) use() performs legacy->canonical mapping and emits a deprecation warning at use-time only. The inner fix_style(s) function contains a hardcoded list of seaborn legacy names and performs s = s.replace('seaborn', 'seaborn-v0_8') after warning, so style.use('seaborn-colorblind') succeeds by mapping to the canonical name before looking up library. Location: lib/matplotlib/style/core.py:use (fix_style). Evidence: fix_style lists seaborn names, calls _api.warn_deprecated(...), then does s = s.replace('seaborn', 'seaborn-v0_8'), and use() subsequently looks up style in library.

4) User-provided style files are merged into the library via update_user_library and update_nested_dict. This merge behavior means user-supplied names (including any legacy-style names provided by users) will be present in the library if defined by the user; the current library population step does not synthesize additional legacy aliases itself. Location: lib/matplotlib/style/core.py:update_user_library and update_nested_dict. Evidence: update_user_library reads user style directories and calls update_nested_dict which uses main_dict.setdefault(name, {}).update(rc_dict).

5) The mapping rule from legacy to canonical is encoded as a literal replacement and a hardcoded legacy-name list inside use(). This creates a coupling between names shipped as files (canonical file stems) and the legacy names recognized at use-time; if one side changes independently of the other they can diverge. Location: lib/matplotlib/style/core.py:use (fix_style) and read_style_directory. Evidence: fix_style performs s.replace('seaborn', 'seaborn-v0_8') and read_style_directory derives keys from file stems.

6) There is no mechanism in the current library object to emit a deprecation warning on direct legacy-key access (plt.style.library['seaborn-colorblind']). The library is a plain dict populated at module load/reload time; there is no custom mapping wrapper in this module that could intercept __getitem__ to warn on deprecated keys. Location: lib/matplotlib/style/core.py (library variable and reload_library). Evidence: library is assigned directly from update_user_library(_base_library) (plain dict) and reload_library does not wrap or replace it with a custom mapping.
StepsToReproduce: Install matplotlib 3.6.1 in a Python 3.9 environment.
Run a Python script with: import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
Then run: the_rc = plt.style.library['seaborn-colorblind']
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Direct lookup plt.style.library['seaborn-colorblind'] returns the rc-parameter mapping corresponding to the 'seaborn-colorblind' style (i.e., the legacy name is present in plt.style.library and yields the same rc mapping as style.use('seaborn-colorblind')).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Accessing plt.style.library['seaborn-colorblind'] raises KeyError: 'seaborn-colorblind' while style.use('seaborn-colorblind') succeeds with a deprecation warning (because use() remaps the legacy name to a canonical name before lookup).

problem_statement.Title: Axis.set_ticks / Axes.set_xticks silently ignore and do not validate text-property kwargs when labels is omitted
problem_statement.Description: When calling Axis.set_ticks (and convenience wrappers like Axes.set_xticks) with text-style kwargs but without passing explicit labels, the kwargs are ignored and not validated. Misspelled or invalid text kwargs (for example, passing xticklabels=...) produce no error and have no effect.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Axis.set_ticks only forwards text-style kwargs to set_ticklabels when the caller provides an explicit 'labels' argument. In the implementation (lib/matplotlib/axis.py:Axis.set_ticks) the code calls self.set_ticklabels(labels, minor=minor, **kwargs) only inside an "if labels is not None" branch, so when labels is None the kwargs are neither validated nor applied.

2) Axis.set_ticklabels applies kwargs to the per-tick Text objects by calling tick.label1._internal_update(kwargs) and tick.label2._internal_update(kwargs) (lib/matplotlib/axis.py:Axis.set_ticklabels). That code path relies on Text/Artist internal validation to surface unknown text properties.

3) The canonical validation routine is Artist._internal_update / Artist._update_props (lib/matplotlib/artist.py). _update_props looks up set_<property> methods on the object and raises AttributeError when a requested property has no corresponding setter; this is the mechanism that causes an error for invalid Text kwargs when labels are provided.

4) Call sites that forward to Axis.set_ticks inherit the same behavior: Axes.set_xticks / set_yticks are thin wrappers created by _axis_method_wrapper (lib/matplotlib/axes/_base.py) that forward to xaxis/yaxis.set_ticks; SecondaryAxis.set_ticks delegates to its internal Axis.set_ticks (lib/matplotlib/axes/_secondary_axes.py); Colorbar.set_ticks forwards to its long axis' set_ticks (lib/matplotlib/colorbar.py). Because the conditional behavior is implemented in Axis.set_ticks, all these callers silently accept/ignore Text-style kwargs when labels is omitted.
StepsToReproduce: Open a Python session with matplotlib imported.
Create an axes: fig, ax = plt.subplots().
Call ax.set_xticks([0, 1], xticklabels=['a', 'b']).
Observe that no exception is raised and the tick labels are not set/changed.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When passing text-style keyword arguments to Axis.set_ticks / Axes.set_xticks, invalid text-property names should be validated and cause an error (e.g., AttributeError). Valid Text-property kwargs should not be silently ignored; at minimum, typos in Text-property kwargs should be surfaced even if explicit labels are not provided.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Passing invalid or misspelled text kwargs (for example, xticklabels) to ax.set_xticks([...], xticklabels=[...]) raises no error and has no effect — the kwargs are ignored when the 'labels' parameter is omitted.

problem_statement.Title: Colormap writes sentinel indices into user arrays causing NumPy 1.24 DeprecationWarning and incorrect indexing
problem_statement.Description: Calling a Colormap with an index array whose dtype cannot represent the colormap's sentinel indices (for under/over/bad) leads Matplotlib to write those sentinel integer values directly into the caller's array. When the caller's array has a small unsigned integer dtype (for example uint8), the sentinel writes overflow/wrap and NumPy 1.24 emits DeprecationWarning(s) about out-of-bound integer-to-integer conversions; the numeric mismatch can also cause incorrect lookup into the colormap lookup table (LUT).
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Sentinel numeric layout is defined in Colormap.__init__ (lib/matplotlib/colors.py):
   - self._i_under = self.N
   - self._i_over = self.N + 1
   - self._i_bad = self.N + 2
   and subclasses allocate lookup tables sized for these sentinels (LUT shape uses self.N + 3). These numeric relationships are explicit in the source and are relied upon by LUT indexing.

2) Colormap.__call__ (lib/matplotlib/colors.py) constructs a local array xa from the user-provided X (xa = np.array(X, copy=True)) and then assigns the sentinel integers directly into xa using expressions:
   - xa[xa > self.N - 1] = self._i_over
   - xa[xa < 0] = self._i_under
   - xa[mask_bad] = self._i_bad
   The code performs these assignments without ensuring that xa's dtype can represent the sentinel integer values (self.N..self.N+2). This encodes the incorrect assumption that xa's dtype will be wide and signed enough to hold those sentinel values; when xa has a narrower or unsigned integer dtype (e.g. uint8), assigning the sentinel integers causes overflow/wrap and triggers NumPy's out-of-bound integer-to-integer conversion warnings.

3) Colormap._set_extremes and the getter methods read/write LUT rows at indices self._i_under/self._i_over/self._i_bad (lib/matplotlib/colors.py). Those methods assume that the sentinel indices written into index arrays retain their numeric values and thus index the intended LUT rows. The mismatch between sentinel numeric values and xa's dtype breaks that expectation and can lead to incorrect under/over/bad color selection.

4) The code computes mask_bad as np.isnan(xa) when X is not a masked array (mask_bad = np.isnan(xa)). This call occurs before any dtype promotion for integer inputs and therefore creates a robustness concern: the behavior of np.isnan on integer-dtype arrays can vary across NumPy versions and was not fully verified in the investigation. The unconditional np.isnan call on xa is therefore an identified uncertainty that affects mask computation for integer or masked-array inputs.
StepsToReproduce: python -c "import matplotlib.pyplot as plt; import numpy as np; plt.get_cmap()(np.empty((0,), dtype=np.uint8))"
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Calling a colormap with an index array of any reasonable numeric dtype (including small unsigned integer dtypes such as uint8) should not produce NumPy DeprecationWarnings about out-of-bound integer-to-integer conversions. Under/over/bad sentinel handling should not overflow or wrap and should return the correct RGBA values for the intended sentinel categories.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: NumPy 1.24 DeprecationWarning(s) are emitted from lib/matplotlib/colors.py when assigning sentinel integer values to the index array (e.g. warnings that conversion of 257 to uint8 will fail). If a caller supplies a small unsigned integer index array, sentinel writes may overflow/wrap (current behavior), causing incorrect colormap lookups for under/over/bad entries in the LUT.

Thanks for the report! Unfortunately I can't reproduce this. What version of numpy are you using when the warning appears?
Sorry, forgot to mention that you need to enable the warnings during normal execution, e.g., `python -W always <file>.py`. In my case, the warnings are issued during `pytest` run which seems to activate these warnings by default.

As for the NumPy version, I'm running
```console
$ python -c 'import numpy; print(numpy.__version__)'
1.24.0
```
Thanks, I can now reproduce 😄 
The problem is that there are three more values, that are by default out of range in this case, to note specific cases:
https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/blob/8d2329ad89120410d7ef04faddba2c51db743b06/lib/matplotlib/colors.py#L673-L675
(N = 256 by default)

These are then assigned to out-of-range and masked/bad values here:
https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/blob/8d2329ad89120410d7ef04faddba2c51db743b06/lib/matplotlib/colors.py#L730-L732
which now raises a deprecation warning.

I think that one way forward would be to check the type of `xa` for int/uint and in that case take modulo the maximum value for `self._i_over` etc. This is basically what happens now anyway, but we need to do it explicitly rather than relying on numpy doing it. (I cannot really see how this makes sense from a color perspective, but we will at least keep the current behavior.)
I think this is exposing a real bug that we need to promote the input data to be bigger than uint8.  What we are doing here is buildin a lookup up table, the first N entries are for for values into the actually color map and then the next 3 entries are the special cases for over/under/bad so `xa` needs to be big enough to hold `self.N + 2` as values.
I don't know if this is a bigger bug or not, but I would like us to have fixed any deprecation warnings from dependencies before 3.7 is out (or if necessary a quick 3.7.1.)

problem_statement.Title: Setting LogNorm on existing colorbar raises ValueError('Invalid vmin or vmax') in matplotlib 3.6.3
problem_statement.Description: When assigning a LogNorm to an existing mappable (for example, plot.norm = LogNorm(...)) after a colorbar has been created, matplotlib 3.6.3 raises ValueError('Invalid vmin or vmax') during draw. The repro uses only positive data and explicit vmin/vmax valid for a log scale; the failure is a regression in 3.6.3 (worked in 3.6.2).
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Colorbar._process_values (lib/matplotlib/colorbar.py::Colorbar._process_values) unconditionally assigns temporary endpoints via the Normalize property setters when the norm is not yet scaled: it executes self.norm.vmin = 0 and self.norm.vmax = 1 (if not self.norm.scaled()). This places the norm into a temporary state with vmin == 0.

2) The Norm instances generated from Scale classes validate their endpoints by applying the scale transform to vmin and vmax and requiring the transformed endpoints be finite. Concretely, the generated Norm code performs: t_vmin, t_vmax = self._trf.transform([self.vmin, self.vmax]); if not np.isfinite([t_vmin, t_vmax]).all(): raise ValueError('Invalid vmin or vmax') (lib/matplotlib/colors.py). If either transformed endpoint is non-finite, the Norm raises ValueError.

3) LogNorm is constructed from LogScale with nonpositive='mask' (lib/matplotlib/colors.py: LogNorm = make_norm_from_scale(functools.partial(scale.LogScale, nonpositive='mask'))(Normalize)). LogTransform, when configured with nonpositive='mask' (i.e., self._clip is False), does not clip nonpositive values to a finite sentinel; transform(0) yields non-finite outputs (lib/matplotlib/scale.py: LogTransform.transform_non_affine). As a result, temporarily setting vmin == 0 causes the scale transform of the endpoints to be non-finite and triggers the ValueError in (2).

4) The problematic sequence in _process_values is: assign vmin=0/vmax=1, call mtransforms.nonsingular on those endpoints, then (for non-boundary norms) call self.norm.inverse(b). Because vmin was set to 0 for a log-based norm, the transform-based validation (2) fails before any subsequent intended adjustment.

5) For contrast, Colorbar._mesh in the same file uses a different pattern when it needs to temporarily change norm endpoints: it sets temporary vmin/vmax inside with self.norm.callbacks.blocked() and cbook._setattr_cm(self.norm, vmin=..., vmax=...) before calling self.norm.inverse(...). That pattern avoids leaving the norm in an invalid transient state (lib/matplotlib/colorbar.py::Colorbar._mesh).

6) A repository search shows the direct assignment self.norm.vmin = 0 in production code appears only in lib/matplotlib/colorbar.py, indicating the hardcoded temporary endpoint assignment is localized to Colorbar._process_values.
StepsToReproduce: Create a 2D array of strictly positive values (e.g. rng.uniform(1,5,(25,25))).
Plot it with pcolormesh and create a colorbar from that mappable (fig.colorbar(plot, ax=ax)).
Set vmin and vmax to positive values valid for log scale (e.g. vmin=1, vmax=5).
Enable interactive drawing (plt.ion()), show the figure, then assign plot.norm = matplotlib.colors.LogNorm(vmin, vmax).
Call plot.autoscale() or allow the figure to redraw; observe the exception during draw.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Assigning a LogNorm to an existing mappable updates the mappable and its colorbar to use the log normalization with the provided vmin/vmax, and redraw completes without raising an exception; the colorbar renders using the log scale and reflects the new vmin/vmax.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: During draw after setting plot.norm = LogNorm(...), matplotlib raises ValueError('Invalid vmin or vmax') (raised from colors.py during Norm.__call__/inverse), preventing the colorbar/mappable from updating. The stack trace shows the ValueError coming from the Norm transform validation.

problem_statement.Title: TypeError when pickling Figure with draggable Legend/Annotation (cannot pickle 'FigureCanvasQTAgg' object)
problem_statement.Description: Calling pickle.dumps(fig) on a Matplotlib Figure that contains a draggable Legend or draggable Annotation raises TypeError: cannot pickle 'FigureCanvasQTAgg' object. The failure occurs because runtime-only GUI objects (the Figure.canvas / FigureCanvas) are indirectly referenced by draggable helper objects attached to artists, and those helpers are included in the pickled state.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. Draggable helper objects capture and store a direct reference to the Figure's backend canvas (a runtime-only GUI object). Location: lib/matplotlib/offsetbox.py:DraggableBase.__init__. Evidence (code patterns): self.canvas = self.ref_artist.figure.canvas and the helper registers callbacks on this canvas (e.g. self.canvas.callbacks._connect_picklable(...)). Incorrect assumption: it is safe for a helper attached to an Artist to retain a direct reference to a non-picklable GUI canvas and therefore be included in the Artist's pickled state.

2. Legend stores the draggable helper on the Legend instance and does not exclude it from pickling. Location: lib/matplotlib/legend.py:Legend (constructor and set_draggable). Evidence (code patterns): self._draggable = None (constructor) and set_draggable assigns self._draggable = DraggableLegend(...) when enabling draggability. Incorrect assumption: the default Artist pickling (Artist.__getstate__) will not include any runtime-only helper fields, so no special handling is needed in Legend.

3. Annotation/Text uses the same pattern and its pickling logic does not remove the helper. Location: lib/matplotlib/text.py:_AnnotationBase and Text.__getstate__. Evidence (code patterns): _AnnotationBase initializes self._draggable and Annotation.draggable assigns DraggableAnnotation(self, ...), while Text.__getstate__ only clears the cached renderer (d['_renderer'] = None) and does not clear or disconnect an existing d['_draggable']. Incorrect assumption: Text/Annotation pickling need only clear renderer caches and does not need to remove runtime-only helpers.

4. Figure.__getstate__ removes only the Figure.canvas attribute but this is insufficient to prevent the canvas from being pickled indirectly. Location: lib/matplotlib/figure.py:Figure.__getstate__. Evidence (code patterns): Figure.__getstate__ does state.pop("canvas") but does not remove references to the canvas that may exist on other objects (artists' runtime helpers). Incorrect assumption: removing the Figure.canvas key from the Figure state guarantees that no FigureCanvas objects will be encountered during pickling of a Figure and its artists.
StepsToReproduce: Run a Python script with Matplotlib (example environment: Windows 10, Matplotlib 3.7.0, Python 3.10).
Create a Figure and Axes, plot some data and add a legend.
Make the legend draggable: leg = ax.legend(); leg.set_draggable(True).
Call pickle.dumps(fig) (or otherwise pickle the Figure).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Calling pickle.dumps(fig) on a Figure with a draggable legend or draggable annotation succeeds (the Figure is serialized) and does not raise a TypeError related to the backend FigureCanvas object.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: pickle.dumps(fig) raises TypeError: cannot pickle 'FigureCanvasQTAgg' object when a Legend or Annotation has been made draggable because a helper attached to the artist holds a direct reference to the Figure canvas and that helper is included in the pickled state.

problem_statement.Title: Figure cannot be pickled after calling Figure.align_labels() (TypeError: cannot pickle 'weakref.ReferenceType')
problem_statement.Description: Calling Figure.align_labels() populates internal label-alignment helpers and afterwards pickle.dumps(fig) raises TypeError: cannot pickle 'weakref.ReferenceType' object. The error occurs when pickling the Figure object after label alignment has been performed.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) lib/matplotlib/cbook.py::Grouper stores weakref.ReferenceType objects directly in its instance state (self._mapping). Evidence in the code: the Grouper constructor and join/get_siblings use weakref.ref(...) for mapping keys and for elements in group lists (e.g. self._mapping = {weakref.ref(x): [weakref.ref(x)] for x in init} and setdefault/get calls with weakref.ref(...)). weakref.ReferenceType objects are not picklable, so leaving them in an object's instance state makes pickling that object raise TypeError.

2) lib/matplotlib/figure.py::FigureBase places cbook.Grouper instances on the Figure instance (self._align_label_groups = {"x": cbook.Grouper(), "y": cbook.Grouper()}) and Figure.align_xlabels / Figure.align_ylabels populate those Grouper instances by calling Grouper.join(...). Because the Grouper objects are attributes of the Figure, their state is included when the Figure is pickled.

3) lib/matplotlib/axis.py::Axis._get_tick_boxes_siblings consumes the Grouper stored on the Figure (it reads from self.figure._align_label_groups and calls grouper.get_siblings(self.axes)). This shows Axis methods rely on the Grouper API and are part of the same root-cause pattern: the non-picklable weakref-based state stored in Grouper propagates through Figure into consumers such as Axis.

4) lib/matplotlib/artist.py::Artist.__getstate__ returns a shallow copy of self.__dict__ (d = self.__dict__.copy(); d['stale_callback'] = None; return d) and does not otherwise sanitize attributes. Because Figure inherits Artist and does not override this pickling behavior for the _align_label_groups attribute, the Grouper instances — and therefore their internal weakref.ReferenceType objects — are included verbatim in the pickled state. The combination of (1) + (2) + (3) + (4) explains why pickle.dumps(fig) raises TypeError after align_labels() has populated the Grouper instances on the Figure.
StepsToReproduce: Create a figure and two subplots: fig = plt.figure(); ax1 = fig.add_subplot(211); ax2 = fig.add_subplot(212)
Plot data and set y-labels on both axes (so labels exist to align).
Call fig.align_labels()
Call pickle.dumps(fig) and observe the TypeError
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: pickle.dumps(fig) completes successfully and returns a pickle (bytes) representing the Figure after align_labels() without raising TypeError.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling pickle.dumps(fig) after fig.align_labels() raises TypeError: cannot pickle 'weakref.ReferenceType' object.

hints_text: As you've noted, pickling is pretty fragile.  Do you _need_ to pickle?

problem_statement.Title: pyplot.clf() called from RangeSlider.on_changed (while dragging) leaves canvas.mouse_grabber pointing to removed Axes and blocks widget input
problem_statement.Description: Calling pyplot.clf()/Figure.clear() from a RangeSlider.on_changed callback (executed while the slider is being dragged and the canvas is grabbed) can clear and remove Axes that still hold the canvas mouse grab. This leaves the canvas in a state where its mouse_grabber attribute remains non-None and points at a removed Axes, which blocks subsequent widget mouse interactions.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) FigureBase.delaxes removes Axes from the figure without inspecting or releasing the canvas mouse grab state. Location: lib/matplotlib/figure.py::FigureBase.delaxes (it calls self._axstack.remove(ax) and self._localaxes.remove(ax) but contains no handling of canvas.mouse_grabber).

2) The canvas grab/release semantics store the current grab-holder in FigureCanvasBase.mouse_grabber: grab_mouse sets self.mouse_grabber = ax and raises if another Axes already grabs the mouse; release_mouse clears mouse_grabber only when self.mouse_grabber is the passed Ax. Location: lib/matplotlib/backend_bases.py::FigureCanvasBase.grab_mouse and ::release_mouse (grab_mouse assigns mouse_grabber, release_mouse does "if self.mouse_grabber is ax: self.mouse_grabber = None").

3) Slider/RangeSlider widget behavior invokes user callbacks while still holding the canvas grab: RangeSlider (and SliderBase) call event.canvas.grab_mouse(self.ax) on press and call observer callbacks (on_changed) from set_val while dragging (grab still active). Location: lib/matplotlib/widgets.py::SliderBase / ::RangeSlider (grab on press; observers.process('changed', ...) is called during set_val).

4) Button behavior differs in timing: Button releases the mouse grab before processing on_clicked observers, so a callback triggered by a button typically runs after the grab is released. Location: lib/matplotlib/widgets.py::Button (release_mouse is called, then _observers.process('clicked', ...)).

5) All code paths that remove Axes via Figure.delaxes (e.g., Figure.clear()/clf which loop over axes and call delaxes, and other removal paths that reach delaxes) are affected by (1) and (2). Locations: lib/matplotlib/figure.py::Figure.clear / ::clf and any code path that calls FigureBase.delaxes. 

These facts together explain the observed behavior: if a widget (RangeSlider) grabs the canvas and then its on_changed callback calls pyplot.clf()/Figure.clear() (which calls delaxes), an Axes that is the current mouse_grabber can be removed without release_mouse being called; mouse_grabber remains non-None and references an Axes that has been removed, blocking future widget mouse interactions.
StepsToReproduce: Run the supplied reproduction script that creates a RangeSlider and a Button and registers on_changed for the slider and on_clicked for the button.
Press and drag the RangeSlider thumb so that the on_changed callback runs while the mouse is held down (the slider grabs the canvas during dragging).
Inside the on_changed callback call pyplot.clf() and pyplot.draw() (as in the supplied reproduction).
After the redraw, try interacting with widgets (e.g., click the button or the slider again) and observe that widget mouse input is unresponsive/blocked.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Calling pyplot.clf()/Figure.clear() from a RangeSlider on_changed callback (during dragging) should not leave the figure in a state that prevents subsequent widget mouse input; widgets should accept mouse interaction as they do when the same sequence is triggered from a Button.on_clicked handler.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When pyplot.clf()/Figure.clear() is called from RangeSlider.on_changed while dragging, subsequent widget mouse input is blocked because the canvas.mouse_grabber remains non-None and references an Axes that has been removed, preventing normal grab/release behavior for later widget interactions.

A can confirm this behavior, but removing and recreating the objects that host the callbacks in the callbacks is definitely on the edge of the intended usage.  

Why are you doing this?  In your application can you get away with not destroying your slider?
