My workaround for the problem was using a button to redraw everything. With that everything is working fine.

That was the weird part for me as they are both callbacks, but in one everything works fine and in the other one all inputs are blocked. If what I'm trying doing is not the intended usage, that's fine for me. 
Thanks for the answer.
The idiomatic way to destructively work on widgets that triggered an event in a UI toolkit is to do the destructive work in an idle callback:
```python
def redraw():
    pyplot.clf()
    addElements()
    pyplot.draw()
    return False

def onchanged(values):
    print("on changed")
    print(values)

```

The line:

```python

```
doesn't work for me. After having a look at the documentation, I think the line should be:

```python
pyplot.gcf().canvas.new_timer(callbacks=[(redraw, tuple(), dict())])
```

But that still didn't work. The redraw callback doesn't seem to trigger.
Sorry, I mean to update that after testing and before posting, but forgot to. That line should be:

Sorry for double posting; now I see that it didn't actually get called!

That's because I forgot to call `start` as well, and the timer was garbage collected at the end of the function. It should be called as you've said, but stored globally and started:
```
import matplotlib.pyplot as pyplot
import matplotlib.widgets as widgets


def redraw():
    print("redraw")
    pyplot.clf()
    addElements()
    pyplot.draw()
    return False


def onchanged(values):
    print("on changed")
    print(values)
    global timer
    timer = pyplot.gcf().canvas.new_timer(callbacks=[(redraw, (), {})])
    timer.start()


def onclick(e):
    print("on click")
    global timer
    timer = pyplot.gcf().canvas.new_timer(callbacks=[(redraw, (), {})])
    timer.start()


def addElements():
    ax = pyplot.axes([0.1, 0.45, 0.8, 0.1])
    global slider
    slider = widgets.RangeSlider(ax, "Test", valmin=1, valmax=10, valinit=(1, 10))
    slider.on_changed(onchanged)
    ax = pyplot.axes([0.1, 0.30, 0.8, 0.1])
    global button
    button = widgets.Button(ax, "Test")
    button.on_clicked(onclick)


addElements()

pyplot.show()
```
Thanks for the answer, the code works without errors, but I'm still able to break it when using the range slider. Then it results in the same problem as my original post. It seems like that happens when triggering the onchanged callback at the same time the timer callback gets called.
Are you sure it's not working, or is it just that it got replaced by a new one that is using the same initial value again? For me, it moves, but once the callback runs, it's reset because the slider is created with `valinit=(1, 10)`.
The code redraws everything fine, but when the onchanged callback of the range slider gets called at the same time as the redraw callback of the timer, I'm not able to give any new inputs to the widgets. You can maybe reproduce that with changing the value of the slider the whole time, while waiting for the redraw.
I think what is happening is that because you are destroying the slider every time the you are also destroying the state of what value you changed it to.  When you create it you re-create it at the default value which produces the effect that you can not seem to change it.  Put another way, the state of what the current value of the slider is is in the `Slider` object.   Replacing the slider object with a new one (correctly) forgets the old value.

-----

I agree it fails in surprising ways, but I think that this is best case "undefined behavior" and worst case incorrect usage of the tools.  In either case there is not much we can do upstream to address this (as if the user _did_ want to get fresh sliders that is not a case we should prevent from happening or warn on).
The "forgetting the value" is not the problem. My problem is, that the input just blocks for every widget in the figure. When that happens, you can click on any widget, but no callback gets triggered. That problem seems to happen when a callback of an object gets called that is/has been destroyed, but I don't know for sure.

But if that is from the incorrect usage of the tools, that's fine for me. I got a decent workaround that works currently, so I just have to keep that in my code for now.

problem_statement.Title: AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'canvas' when using mplcursors with matplotlib 3.7.1
problem_statement.Description: Clicking several points when using mplcursors with matplotlib 3.7.1 can raise an AttributeError originating from matplotlib.offsetbox.DraggableBase; after the exception appears, further selections produce the same traceback and selection behavior is broken.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) DraggableBase.canvas assumes the referenced artist is always parented to a Figure. Location: lib/matplotlib/offsetbox.py::DraggableBase.canvas — defined as canvas = property(lambda self: self.ref_artist.figure.canvas). Evaluating this property raises AttributeError when self.ref_artist.figure is None.

2) DraggableBase.__init__ dereferences the canvas property at construction time and registers callbacks on that canvas (reads self.canvas.supports_blit and calls self.canvas.callbacks._connect_picklable). Location: lib/matplotlib/offsetbox.py::DraggableBase.__init__. This encodes the assumption that a canvas is available at init time.

3) DraggableBase._check_still_parented calls self.disconnect() when the artist has no figure, but disconnect re-evaluates the canvas property and therefore attempts to access ref_artist.figure.canvas again. Location: lib/matplotlib/offsetbox.py::DraggableBase._check_still_parented and ::disconnect. When ref_artist.figure is None this re-evaluation raises the AttributeError observed in the traceback.

4) connect/disconnect operate by re-dereferencing ref_artist.figure.canvas rather than holding the same canvas object instance used during connect. Location: lib/matplotlib/offsetbox.py::DraggableBase.__init__ and ::disconnect. The code assumes the canvas property can be dereferenced later instead of relying on a cached canvas reference.

5) Event handlers use canvas methods without guarding for a missing canvas: on_pick, on_motion, and on_release call canvas methods (draw, copy_from_bbox, restore_region, blit) and create/disconnect motion callbacks via canvas.callbacks without additional checks for a present Figure/canvas. Location: lib/matplotlib/offsetbox.py::DraggableBase.on_pick, ::on_motion, ::on_release. These calls can raise when ref_artist.figure becomes None.

6) Subclasses that rely on DraggableBase for callback management are affected by the same assumptions. Location: lib/matplotlib/offsetbox.py::DraggableOffsetBox and ::DraggableAnnotation — they inherit DraggableBase behavior and thus share the root-cause pattern.

7) Exceptions raised inside callback invocations are forwarded to Matplotlib's callback registry exception handler, which by default prints the traceback. Location: lib/matplotlib/cbook.py::CallbackRegistry.process. The AttributeError raised while processing a callback is therefore printed to the user and leaves the callback state inconsistent.
StepsToReproduce: Run the following minimal script with matplotlib 3.7.1 and mplcursors installed:

import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import mplcursors as mpl
x = np.arange(1, 11)
y1 = x
plt.scatter(x, y1)
mpl.cursor()
plt.show()
In the displayed figure, click a few data points (several clicks).
Observe that after some clicks a Python traceback is printed and subsequent point selections produce the same traceback.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Selecting points with mplcursors does not raise exceptions or print tracebacks; repeated selections work normally and no internal AttributeError is shown to the user.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: After clicking a few points a traceback is printed showing AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'canvas' (originating from offsetbox.DraggableBase.canvas property). After this, subsequent selections trigger the same traceback and selection behavior is broken.

problem_statement.Title: Colorbar fails to update after replacing mappable.norm with uninitialized LogNorm — ZeroDivisionError
problem_statement.Description: Replacing a mappable's norm with an uninitialized LogNorm (vmin/vmax unset) leaves the colorbar with an uninitialized norm; update_normal has no visible effect and update_bruteforce triggers a ZeroDivisionError when the colorbar forces a 0..1 fallback and calls Norm.inverse.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) Colorbar._process_values (lib/matplotlib/colorbar.py) unconditionally assigns an unscaled norm to vmin=0 and vmax=1: self.norm.vmin = 0; self.norm.vmax = 1. This encodes the incorrect assumption that a 0..1 fallback is safe for all Norm subclasses. 2) Colorbar.update_normal (lib/matplotlib/colorbar.py) assigns self.norm = mappable.norm when the mappable's norm changes but does not initialize the new norm from the mappable's data (it does not call mappable.autoscale_None() / self.norm.autoscale_None(self._A)). This leaves new Norm instances (e.g., LogNorm()) uninitialized. 3) LogNorm.inverse (lib/matplotlib/colors.py) performs arithmetic that divides by vmin using the formula: return vmin * ma.power((vmax / vmin), val). If vmin == 0 (set by the fallback in _process_values) this causes ZeroDivisionError. 4) Colorbar._process_values calls mtransforms.nonsingular(...) after setting the 0..1 fallback, but nonsingular does not guarantee a positive vmin suitable for LogNorm; the code therefore assumes nonsingular's result is valid for all Norms, which is false for logarithmic norms. 5) The intended initialization path exists: ScalarMappable.autoscale_None (lib/matplotlib/cm.py) calls self.norm.autoscale_None(self._A) to populate norm.vmin/vmax from the mappable's data; update_normal currently omits this step, encoding the incorrect assumption that assigning the norm object alone is sufficient. 6) Investigation gaps: the agent did not locate an implementation for update_bruteforce in the inspected codebase and did not validate autoscale behavior for arrays containing zeros or negatives (autoscale may still yield norm.vmin <= 0 for LogNorm), so additional validation paths may still produce invalid vmin values.
StepsToReproduce: Create and run a script with the following contents:

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.colors import LogNorm
import numpy as np

img = 10**np.random.normal(1, 1, size=(50, 50))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 1)
plot = ax.imshow(img, cmap='gray')
cb = fig.colorbar(plot, ax=ax)
plot.norm = LogNorm()
cb.update_normal(plot)  # no effect
cb.update_bruteforce(plot)  # raises ZeroDivisionError
plt.show()
Observe that cb.update_normal(plot) does not change the colorbar and that cb.update_bruteforce(plot) raises a ZeroDivisionError.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: After replacing a mappable's norm (for example setting plot.norm = LogNorm()), the colorbar updates to reflect the new norm (log scaling and tick/colormap mapping corresponding to the data) without raising exceptions.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Setting plot.norm = LogNorm() then calling cb.update_normal(plot) leaves the colorbar unchanged. Calling cb.update_bruteforce(plot) leads to a ZeroDivisionError (traceback shows matplotlib/colors.py LogNorm.inverse computing return vmin * ma.power((vmax / vmin), val) with vmin == 0).

problem_statement.Title: Shared-axis propagation suppresses xlim_changed callbacks on sibling Axes
problem_statement.Description: When an Axes' limits change and that change is propagated to sibling Axes that share the axis (sharex/sharey), the sibling Axes do not run their registered "xlim_changed"/"ylim_changed" callbacks. The initiating Axes' callbacks run, but observers registered on shared sibling Axes are not invoked.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) lib/matplotlib/axis.py:Axis._set_lim — when emit is True the method processes callbacks only on the initiating Axes via self.axes.callbacks.process(f"{name}lim_changed", self.axes), and then propagates the new limits to sibling Axes by calling their internal axis setter with emit=False (other._axis_map[name]._set_lim(v0, v1, emit=False, ...)). Because the sibling setter is called with emit=False, the sibling's own CallbackRegistry is not processed in that call path and observers registered on the sibling Axes are not invoked.

2) lib/matplotlib/axis.py:_get_shared_axes and lib/matplotlib/axis.py:_get_axis_name — the sibling enumeration and the generic axis-name mechanism are the pathways used by the propagation (the code enumerates siblings via self.axes._shared_axes[self._get_axis_name()].get_siblings(self.axes) and uses name = self._get_axis_name() to construct the signal f"{name}lim_changed"). These locations participate in the propagation that suppresses sibling callbacks and should be considered part of the root-cause pattern.

3) lib/matplotlib/axes/_base.py:_AxesBase.sharex and sharey — during the creation of sharing, the implementation calls set_xlim/set_ylim with emit=False (e.g., self.set_xlim(x0, x1, emit=False, ...)), which intentionally suppresses callbacks at share setup time. That suppression is present in the same API parameter (emit) used by Axis._set_lim for propagation.

4) lib/matplotlib/cbook.py:CallbackRegistry.process — callback invocation is performed per-object via each Axes' CallbackRegistry (CallbackRegistry.process(signal, *args)). Axis._set_lim invokes the initiating Axes' registry (self.axes.callbacks.process(...)) but the propagation path does not invoke the sibling Axes' registry, creating an asymmetry between the initiating Axes and its shared siblings with respect to callback notification.
StepsToReproduce: Create two Axes that share the x-axis, e.g. fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(2, sharex=True).
Register a callback on the sibling Axes: ax2.callbacks.connect('xlim_changed', lambda ax: print('ax2 xlim changed')).
Change the limits on the initiating Axes: ax1.set_xlim(0, 10) (or interactively zoom/pan ax1).
Observe that the callback registered on ax2 is not invoked.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When an Axes' limits change, every affected Axes (including siblings that share the axis) should invoke their registered 'xlim_changed'/'ylim_changed' callbacks so observers on those Axes are notified of the change.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Only the initiating Axes' 'xlim_changed'/'ylim_changed' callbacks are invoked. Sibling Axes updated via shared-axis propagation do not run their registered limit-change callbacks.

I'm also seeing this behavior on matplotlib 3.4.1.  Working from the [resampling data example](https://matplotlib.org/stable/gallery/event_handling/resample.html), I've been developing an adaptive waveform plotter in [this PR](https://github.com/librosa/librosa/issues/1207) (code included there).  The specific quirks that I'm seeing are as follows:

- Create two axes with shared x axis (eg, `fig, (ax0, ax1) = plt.subplots(nrows=2, sharex=True)`), and set an axis callback on `ax0` for `xlim_changed`.  If the xlim changes on `ax1`, which does not directly have the callback set, the axes still update appropriately but the callback is never triggered.
- Possibly related: if the callback is set on `ax0` first, and some time later we draw on `ax1`, the callback never triggers even if we directly set the xlims on `ax0`.

Note: if I create the shared axes, draw on `ax1` first and set the callback on `ax0` last, everything works as expected.  So I don't think there's any fundamental incompatibility here.  It does seem like some data structure is being either ignored or clobbered though.
A short self-contained example would be very helpful here!  Thanks  
"short" is relative here :)  There is a full setup in the linked PR, but here's something hopefully a little more streamlined:

```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# From https://matplotlib.org/stable/gallery/event_handling/resample.html
# A class that will downsample the data and recompute when zoomed.
class DataDisplayDownsampler:
    def __init__(self, xdata, ydata):
        self.origYData = ydata
        self.origXData = xdata
        self.max_points = 50
        self.delta = xdata[-1] - xdata[0]

    def downsample(self, xstart, xend):
        # get the points in the view range
        mask = (self.origXData > xstart) & (self.origXData < xend)
        # dilate the mask by one to catch the points just outside
        # of the view range to not truncate the line
        mask = np.convolve([1, 1, 1], mask, mode='same').astype(bool)
        # sort out how many points to drop
        ratio = max(np.sum(mask) // self.max_points, 1)

        # mask data
        xdata = self.origXData[mask]
        ydata = self.origYData[mask]

        # downsample data
        xdata = xdata[::ratio]
        ydata = ydata[::ratio]

        print("using {} of {} visible points".format(len(ydata), np.sum(mask)))

        return xdata, ydata

    def update(self, ax):
        # Update the line
        lims = ax.viewLim
        if abs(lims.width - self.delta) > 1e-8:
            self.delta = lims.width
            xstart, xend = lims.intervalx
            self.line.set_data(*self.downsample(xstart, xend))
            ax.figure.canvas.draw_idle()


# Create a signal

ydata = np.sin(2*np.pi*xdata/153) + np.cos(2*np.pi*xdata/127)


# --- This does not work: ax1 drawn after ax0 kills callbacks
```

problem_statement.Title: AxesGrid construction fails with non-default axes_class (.axis assumed subscriptable)
problem_statement.Description: Creating an AxesGrid with a non-default Axes class (for example a cartopy GeoAxes instance used as axes_class) can raise TypeError: 'method' object is not subscriptable during AxesGrid initialization. The failure occurs when the toolkit code indexes ax.axis (e.g. ax.axis['bottom']) and assumes an AxisDict-like API that provides per-side objects with a .toggle(...) method.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) In lib/mpl_toolkits/axes_grid1/axes_grid.py::_tick_only: the code assumes ax.axis is subscriptable and supports expressions like ax.axis['bottom'].toggle(...). The function directly does ax.axis['bottom'].toggle(ticklabels=..., label=...) and ax.axis['left'].toggle(...); this encodes the assumption that the Axes instance exposes an AxisDict-like mapping on its axis attribute.

2) In lib/mpl_toolkits/axes_grid1/axes_grid.py:CbarAxesBase.toggle_label: the code assumes self.axis is subscriptable and that self.axis[self.orientation].toggle(...) exists. The method assigns axis = self.axis[self.orientation] and then calls axis.toggle(...), which is the same AxisDict expectation as in _tick_only.

3) The toolkit's own Axes implementation (lib/mpl_toolkits/axes_grid1/mpl_axes.py) provides an AxisDict and SimpleAxisArtist objects that implement .toggle(). The toolkit therefore works with its own default Axes because those Axes expose axis[...] semantics; however, that AxisDict API is not guaranteed for all Axes subclasses in the broader Matplotlib ecosystem.

4) Mismatch with non-default / third-party Axes: when an Axes instance used as axes_class exposes axis as a callable method (i.e., axis is not a subscriptable mapping), attempting ax.axis['bottom'] results in TypeError: 'method' object is not subscriptable. The user's runtime traceback shows this exact TypeError originating from the ax.axis[...] line in _tick_only, indicating that in that run the Axes.axis attribute was a method rather than an AxisDict. I did not inspect cartopy source in this environment, so the assertion about cartopy is based on the user's runtime evidence rather than a direct code quote from cartopy.
StepsToReproduce: Run Python with cartopy and matplotlib installed.
Create and run a script containing the minimal example:

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from cartopy.crs import PlateCarree
from cartopy.mpl.geoaxes import GeoAxes
from mpl_toolkits.axes_grid1 import AxesGrid
fig = plt.figure()
axes_class = (GeoAxes, dict(map_projection=PlateCarree()))
gr = AxesGrid(fig, 111, nrows_ncols=(1,1), axes_class=axes_class)
Observe that AxesGrid construction raises a TypeError during initialization.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Constructing an AxesGrid with a non-default axes_class (for example a cartopy GeoAxes) should not raise a TypeError during normal initialization. The label-mode logic that enables/disables ticklabels and axis labels for grid axes should either succeed or be a no-op for axes classes that do not provide the toolkit's AxisDict API, but must not raise a TypeError during construction.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: A traceback is raised during AxesGrid initialization ending with TypeError: 'method' object is not subscriptable. The exception originates from code that evaluates ax.axis['bottom'] in lib/mpl_toolkits/axes_grid1/axes_grid.py::_tick_only (which is invoked from Grid.set_label_mode during AxesGrid initialization) when axes_class is a third-party Axes instance whose .axis attribute is a method rather than a mapping.

@jklymak the reason why I went to use AxesGrid was because it seemed the easiest for me to create multiple GeoAxes instances flexibly (i.e. with or without colorbar axes, and with flexible location of those) and with an easy control of both horizonal and vertical padding of GeoAxis instances and independently, of the colorbar axes, also because the aspect of maps (lat / lon range) tends to mess with the alignment. 
I know that this can all be solved via subplots or GridSpec, etc., but I found that AxisGrid was the most simple way to do this (after trying other options and always ending up having overlapping axes ticklabels or too large padding between axes, etc.). AxesGrid seems to be made for my problem and it was very easy for me to set up a subplot grid meeting my needs for e.g. plotting 12 monthly maps of climate model data with proper padding, etc. 
![multimap_example](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/12813228/78986627-f310dc00-7b2b-11ea-8d47-a5c90b68171d.png)

The code I used to create initiate this figure is based on the example from the cartopy website that @QuLogic mentions above:

```python
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(18, 7))
axes_class = (GeoAxes, dict(map_projection=ccrs.PlateCarree()))
axgr = AxesGrid(fig, 111, axes_class=axes_class,
                    nrows_ncols=(3, 4),
                    axes_pad=(0.6, 0.5), # control padding separately for e.g. colorbar labels, axes titles, etc.
                    cbar_location='right',
                    cbar_mode="each",
                    cbar_pad="5%",
                    cbar_size='3%',
                    label_mode='') 

# here follows the plotting code of the displayed climate data using pyaerocom by loading a 2010 monthly example model dataset, looping over the (GeoAxes, cax) instances of the grid and calling pyaerocom.plot.mapping.plot_griddeddata_on_map on the monthly subsets.
```

However, @jklymak I was not aware of the `constrained_layout` option in `subplots` and indeed, looking at [the constrained layout guide](https://matplotlib.org/3.2.1/tutorials/intermediate/constrainedlayout_guide.html), this seems to provide the control needed to not mess with padding / spacing etc. I will try this out for my problem. Nonetheless, since cartopy refers to the AxesGrid option, it may be good if this issue could be fixed in any case.

Also, constrained layout itself is declared experimental in the above guide and may be deprecated, so it may be a bit uncertain for users and developers that build upon matplotlib, what option to go for.


@jgliss Yeah, I think I agree that `axes_grid` is useful to pack subplots together that have a certain aspect ratio.  Core matplotlib takes the opposite approach and puts the white space between the axes, `axes_grid` puts the space around the axes.  

I agree with @anntzer comment above (https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/issues/17069#issuecomment-611635018), but feel that we should move axes_grid into core matplotlib and change the API as we see fit when we do so.  

I also agree that its time `constrained_layout` drops its experimental tag. 

Back on topic, though, this seems to be a regression and we should fix it.
